stem: An 11-year-old girl presents to ED with 3 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia and 4 kg weight loss. She has no vomiting, abdominal pain or tachypnoea. She walks into the cubicle. A fingerprick capillary BSL is 22 mmol/L. You are deciding the initial inpatient plan as the paediatric registrar. Investigations are shown.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 96, RR 18, BP 108/64 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, mildly dry mucous membranes, abdomen soft, no Kussmaul breathing, no acetone on breath
data_table.Investigations: Venous pH 7.35, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 1.6 mmol/L, BGL 22 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 60 micromol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Admit and commence a subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin regimen with diabetes education and oral fluids
options.rationale: Normal venous pH, bicarbonate above 18 mmol/L and beta-hydroxybutyrate under 3 mmol/L exclude DKA; new T1DM without ketoacidosis is managed with subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin, oral hydration and structured education. (Source: APEG/ADS New Onset T1DM)

options.text: Commence intravenous insulin infusion at 0.05 unit/kg/h with 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rates
options.rationale: Insulin infusion is reserved for DKA or unable to tolerate oral intake; in mild hyperglycaemia without acidosis it adds risk of hypoglycaemia without benefit. (Source: APEG/ADS New Onset T1DM)

options.text: Administer a 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus then commence DKA protocol with two-bag system
options.rationale: She does not meet DKA criteria; aggressive resuscitation in well-perfused euglycaemic-ketosis risks cerebral oedema and is not warranted. (Source: APEG/ADS DKA)

options.text: Discharge after diabetes nurse contact and commence metformin pending outpatient endocrinology review
options.rationale: Metformin is not first-line for type 1 diabetes; insulin is required from diagnosis. New T1DM mandates inpatient education and insulin initiation. (Source: APEG/ADS New Onset T1DM)

options.text: Trial oral rehydration in the short-stay unit and re-measure ketones at 4 hours before deciding on insulin
options.rationale: Insulin should not be delayed in new T1DM; without insulin ketosis will worsen and DKA may develop overnight. (Source: APEG/ADS New Onset T1DM)

explanation.summary: New onset type 1 diabetes without ketoacidosis is managed with subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin, oral hydration and structured inpatient education in Australian paediatric practice. DKA criteria require venous pH under 7.30 or bicarbonate under 18 mmol/L plus ketonaemia. Intravenous insulin and DKA protocols are reserved for those meeting these biochemical criteria or who cannot tolerate oral intake. Cerebral oedema risk increases with aggressive fluid resuscitation in mildly ill children, making the distinction important.
key_points: DKA requires pH under 7.30 OR bicarbonate under 18 mmol/L AND ketones 3 mmol/L or higher
Subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin is first-line for new T1DM without DKA
Metformin has no role in initial type 1 diabetes management
explanation.pearls: Typical starting total daily dose is 0.5 to 0.75 unit/kg/day split as 50% basal, 50% bolus with carbohydrate-counting and correction doses titrated to BSL targets.

stem: A 2-year-old boy is recovering from his second culture-confirmed febrile UTI in 6 months, both with E. coli. His first UTI at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and he had a normal renal tract ultrasound at the time. Today he is afebrile after 24 hours of oral co-trimoxazole. His mother asks what additional imaging is needed.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0 degrees Celsius, HR 110, RR 24, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well, abdomen soft non-tender, no renal angle tenderness, growth on 50th centile, no spinal anomaly

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate imaging plan?

options.text: Commence long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis and defer all imaging unless a further symptomatic infection occurs in the future
options.rationale: The RCH Melbourne pathway reserves prophylaxis for proven high-grade reflux or frequent recurrence and does not defer imaging review in recurrent UTI. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection)
source_refs: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection

options.text: Arrange an urgent inpatient micturating cystourethrogram to assess for vesico-ureteric reflux before discharge from this admission
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive and is reserved for atypical infection or abnormal renal tract ultrasound; it is not the RCH first step after a second uncomplicated febrile UTI. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection)
source_refs: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection

options.text: Repeat renal tract ultrasound and refer to paediatric nephrology for recurrent UTI; no routine DMSA scan in the RCH pathway
options.rationale: The RCH Melbourne CPG recommends a non-urgent renal tract ultrasound for recurrent UTI together with paediatric specialist referral; DMSA is part of the NICE NG224 algorithm and is not part of the RCH cascade. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection)
source_refs: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection

options.text: Arrange a contrast-enhanced CT KUB to characterise the renal cortex and to exclude any obstructive uropathy in this child
options.rationale: CT delivers significant ionising radiation and has no routine role in screening children for scarring or reflux. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection)
source_refs: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection

options.text: Arrange a DMSA scan in 4 to 6 months to assess for renal cortical scarring after this second febrile UTI episode
options.rationale: Would be correct only when following NICE NG224 rather than the local RCH Melbourne pathway; the RCH CPG does not include routine DMSA in the recurrent UTI cascade. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection)
source_refs: RCH Melbourne CPG: Urinary tract infection

explanation.summary: After a second febrile UTI in a child under 3 years, the RCH Melbourne CPG recommends a non-urgent renal tract ultrasound and paediatric specialist referral. DMSA at 4 to 6 months is a NICE NG224 recommendation and is not part of the RCH algorithm. MCUG is reserved for atypical infection or abnormal renal tract ultrasound.
key_points: RCH Melbourne recurrent-UTI imaging: non-urgent renal tract ultrasound and specialist referral
DMSA is a NICE construct not adopted by RCH
MCUG reserved for atypical infection or abnormal ultrasound
Prophylaxis only for proven high-grade reflux or frequent recurrence
explanation.pearls: The RCH bottom-up strategy of ultrasound plus clinical follow-up reduces invasive imaging without missing significant uropathy.

sources.label: NICE NG: Urinary tract infection in under 16s

stem: A 2-day-old male term neonate is brought back to a regional hospital with poor feeding and cyanosis. He was born by uncomplicated NVD with Apgars 9 and 10 and discharged at 18 hours. His mother reports he has become progressively bluer over the morning, particularly with crying. Pre-ductal SpO2 is 78% and post-ductal SpO2 is 78%; both fail to rise above 85% in 100% oxygen by Hudson mask. Cardiac auscultation reveals a single second heart sound and no murmur. The paediatric retrieval team is 2 hours away.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 168, RR 60, SpO2 78% pre and post-ductal, BP 64/40 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Centrally cyanosed even on 100% oxygen, peripheries warm with brisk capillary refill, abdomen soft, liver edge 1 cm below costal margin, no audible murmur, single loud S2
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30, bicarbonate 19 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L, BSL 3.2 mmol/L; CXR shows narrow mediastinum with mildly increased pulmonary vascularity

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step before retrieval arrives?

options.text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion at 10 nanogram/kg/min and monitor for apnoea
options.rationale: Failure of SpO2 to rise above 85% in 100% oxygen with single S2 and narrow mediastinum suggests duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease (transposition with intact septum being a key consideration); prostaglandin E1 maintains ductal patency and is life-saving while awaiting retrieval. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Continue 100% oxygen via Hudson mask and arrange urgent transthoracic echocardiogram before any treatment
options.rationale: Oxygen alone is insufficient in duct-dependent lesions and may further close the ductus; treatment should not await imaging when failure of hyperoxia is documented. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Intubate, ventilate with 100% oxygen and commence dopamine infusion at 10 microgram/kg/min
options.rationale: Without ductal patency, dopamine will not raise systemic oxygenation; high FiO2 may accelerate ductal closure and worsen cyanosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence intravenous adenosine 100 microgram/kg as a rapid bolus for suspected supraventricular tachycardia
options.rationale: Heart rate 168 in a neonate is appropriate for distress and not SVT; adenosine has no role in cyanotic congenital heart disease without arrhythmia. (Source: ANZCOR Neonatal Resuscitation)

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for sepsis and defer cardiac assessment until cultures return
options.rationale: Sepsis is in the differential but the hyperoxia test result and cardiac signs point to duct-dependent cardiac lesion; delaying prostaglandin while awaiting cultures risks ductal closure and circulatory collapse. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

explanation.summary: A neonate with central cyanosis that fails to rise above 85% in 100% oxygen, single second heart sound and narrow mediastinum on CXR has duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease until proven otherwise. Intravenous prostaglandin E1 at 10 to 50 nanogram/kg/min is started immediately to maintain ductal patency while awaiting paediatric cardiac retrieval. Apnoea, hypotension and fever are recognised side effects requiring monitoring.
key_points: Hyperoxia test failure (SpO2 under 85% in 100% O2) suggests cyanotic congenital heart disease
Prostaglandin E1 10 nanogram/kg/min maintains ductal patency and is started before imaging
Apnoea is a key adverse effect; have intubation ready when starting prostaglandin
explanation.pearls: Pre and post-ductal saturations equal at 78% with the single S2 fits transposition of the great arteries with intact septum; the patient depends on shunting at atrial and ductal level until balloon atrial septostomy or arterial switch surgery.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 woman at 8 weeks by LMP presents to ED with 12 hours of light vaginal bleeding and mild cramping. She has no fever, no foul discharge and no shoulder-tip pain. She is Rh D positive. A transvaginal ultrasound shows an intrauterine sac with an embryo of crown-rump length 16 mm and a fetal heart rate of 160 bpm. There is a small subchorionic haematoma. The cervix is closed on examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 84, RR 16, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft non-tender, cervix closed on speculum with small amount of blood in vault, no adnexal tenderness, uterus consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 64,000 IU/L; haemoglobin 124 g/L; blood group A Rh D positive

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Offer misoprostol 800 microgram vaginally as outpatient medical management of likely miscarriage
options.rationale: Misoprostol is used for confirmed miscarriage, not threatened miscarriage with a viable fetus; offering it here would terminate a viable pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 400 mg twice daily until 16 weeks to prevent miscarriage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone has a modest benefit only in women with bleeding and a previous miscarriage; routine use for first-episode threatened miscarriage is not recommended in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscularly to prevent maternal alloimmunisation
options.rationale: She is Rh D positive so anti-D is not required; routine first-trimester anti-D is not indicated in Rh-positive women. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Admit overnight for bed rest, intravenous fluids and serial beta-hCG measurements before discharge
options.rationale: Bed rest does not improve outcomes in threatened miscarriage and admission with serial hCG adds no value when a viable intrauterine pregnancy has already been confirmed. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Reassure her, discharge home with safety-net advice and arrange GP follow-up in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage with confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy, closed cervix and stable observations is managed expectantly with outpatient follow-up and safety-net advice; no progesterone or admission is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage with bleeding, closed cervix and a confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy is managed expectantly with safety-net advice and outpatient GP follow-up. Vaginal progesterone has only modest benefit in women with previous miscarriage and bleeding; routine use is not recommended. Anti-D is not required in Rh-positive women. Misoprostol and surgical management are reserved for confirmed pregnancy loss.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage with viable IUP and closed cervix is managed expectantly
Anti-D is not required in Rh-positive women
Vaginal progesterone is reserved for bleeding plus previous miscarriage
explanation.pearls: Subchorionic haematoma greater than 25% of gestational sac volume is associated with a slightly higher miscarriage rate but does not change initial expectant management.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with an intact uterus and no menstruation for 14 months presents to her GP for severe daily vasomotor symptoms disrupting sleep and work. She has no history of breast cancer, VTE, ischaemic heart disease or migraine with aura. Her BMI is 26 kg/m^2 and BP is 122/76 mmHg. A recent FBC, lipid profile and breast examination are normal. She declines non-hormonal therapies and asks about menopausal hormone therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well, no breast lump, no thyromegaly, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date and negative; mammography normal in past 12 months

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate MHT regimen?

options.text: Topical low-dose vaginal oestrogen alone for systemic vasomotor symptoms and sleep disturbance
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms only and does not control systemic vasomotor symptoms. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Oestradiol-only patch without a progestogen because she is past her final menstrual period
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in women with an intact uterus increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer regardless of menopausal status; a progestogen is required. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Sequential combined regimen with monthly progestogen withdrawal bleeds for the first year of treatment
options.rationale: Sequential regimens are used in perimenopausal women still having spontaneous cycles; once she is more than a year post final menses, continuous combined therapy is preferred. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg orally daily as first-line treatment for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms
options.rationale: Tibolone is an alternative when standard MHT is unsuitable; it is not preferred first-line over continuous combined therapy in women without specific contraindications. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Continuous combined oestradiol with micronised progesterone for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Women with an intact uterus who are more than 12 months post final menstrual period are offered continuous combined oestradiol with progestogen to protect the endometrium and avoid scheduled withdrawal bleeds. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

explanation.summary: A postmenopausal woman with an intact uterus and disabling vasomotor symptoms is offered systemic menopausal hormone therapy. Once 12 months past the final menstrual period, continuous combined oestrogen with progestogen is preferred to avoid bleeds and provide endometrial protection. Unopposed oestrogen is contraindicated with an intact uterus.
key_points: Continuous combined MHT is preferred more than 12 months post final menses
Progestogen is mandatory with oestrogen for women with an intact uterus
Vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms only and is not systemic therapy
explanation.pearls: Micronised progesterone (Prometrium) has a more favourable breast and venous safety profile than older synthetic progestins and is widely used in Australia.
